era_context: The High Middle Ages in Europe; the Gregorian Reform movement is well-established. The Jin Dynasty is consolidating power in northern China; the Song Dynasty controls the south. The Seljuk Empire is at its height. Islamic civilization flourishes across the Islamic world, with significant intellectual and scientific activity.

events.title: Antipope Anacletus II dies; end of papal schism
events.description: Antipope Anacletus II died on 25 January 1138 (not 1130, but the schism had been ongoing since 1130). In 1130, the papal schism between Pope Innocent II and Antipope Anacletus II was at its height. Anacletus II, supported by the powerful Pierleoni family, had been elected as an alternative pope, creating a major crisis in the Church. The schism lasted until Anacletus's death in 1138, but the conflict was already well-established by 1130.
events.certainty_note: The papal schism of 1130–1138 is well-documented in ecclesiastical records. The election of Anacletus II in 1130 is confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Norman Kingdom of Sicily established under Roger II
events.description: Roger II of Sicily was crowned King of Sicily on Christmas Day 1130, establishing the Norman Kingdom of Sicily. This was a major political event in medieval Europe, as it created a powerful new kingdom that would dominate the Mediterranean for centuries. Roger II was the son of Roger I (the Norman conqueror of Sicily) and had inherited the county of Sicily. His coronation as king marked the elevation of the Norman principality to a major European power. The kingdom would become a center of cultural and intellectual exchange between Christian, Islamic, and Jewish civilizations.

sources.name: Liudprand of Cremona and other Italian chronicles

events.certainty_note: Roger II's coronation as King of Sicily on Christmas Day 1130 is well-documented in multiple independent sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Song Dynasty military and administrative consolidation
events.description: Emperor Gaozong of Song (r. 1127–1162) was consolidating power and rebuilding the Song state after the Jin invasion and the loss of northern territories. Administrative reforms, military reorganization, and economic recovery were ongoing. The Song court was establishing itself in the south, and Gaozong was working to stabilize the dynasty and prevent further Jin expansion. This period saw the development of new military technologies and tactics.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian and Song official histories
Song administrative records and military treatises

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty records are extensive, but specific events in 1130 are documented in official histories compiled after the fact.

events.title: Bernard of Clairvaux's influence on European monasticism and theology
events.description: Bernard of Clairvaux (1090–1153) was at the height of his influence in 1130. He was the leading figure of the Cistercian order and one of the most influential theologians and spiritual leaders of the age. His writings on mystical theology, his role as a papal advisor, and his promotion of the Cistercian order were shaping European religious life. Bernard's emphasis on personal spiritual experience and his critique of scholastic rationalism were influential in the broader intellectual debates of the time.
key_figures: Bernard of Clairvaux (Cistercian abbot and theologian)

sources.name: Bernard's own writings (sermons, letters, theological works)

events.certainty_note: Bernard of Clairvaux's life and work are extensively documented through his own writings and contemporary monastic records.

events.title: Islamic intellectual and scientific activity across the Islamic world
events.region: Islamic world (Al-Andalus, Persia, Iraq, Syria, Egypt)
events.description: The 12th century was a period of remarkable intellectual and scientific activity in the Islamic world. In Al-Andalus, scholars were engaged in mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and philosophy. In the eastern Islamic world, similar activities were underway. This period saw the translation and transmission of Greek and Islamic scientific knowledge, which would eventually reach Europe through Al-Andalus and the Crusader States. The work of scholars in this period was laying the groundwork for the scientific revolution of later centuries.

sources.name: Later Islamic biographical and historiographical sources

events.certainty_note: Islamic intellectual activity in the 12th century is well-documented, but specific events in 1130 are not precisely dated. The broader context of scientific and philosophical advancement is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The Norman Kingdom of Sicily is sometimes portrayed as a purely Christian kingdom, but in fact it was a multicultural and multireligious state that incorporated Islamic, Jewish, and Christian elements. Roger II employed Muslim administrators and scholars, and the kingdom became a center of cultural exchange. This complicates the narrative of a simple Christian conquest and suggests a more nuanced picture of medieval Mediterranean politics. Additionally, the papal schism of 1130–1138 is sometimes presented as a minor event, but it was actually a major crisis that divided the Church and had significant political consequences.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1130 is strong for European ecclesiastical and political history, good for Song Dynasty China, weaker for the Islamic world and Scandinavia, and essentially absent for most of Africa and the Americas. The sources are heavily weighted toward elite institutions and literate cultures. The establishment of the Norman Kingdom of Sicily is well-documented because it was of great political and religious significance, but the lives of common people, including the Muslim and Jewish populations of Sicily, are largely invisible in the surviving records. The papal schism is well-documented in ecclesiastical records, but the impact on ordinary Christians is not well-attested.

graph_edges.note: The papal schism that began in 1130 with Anacletus II's election led to his death in 1138 and the end of the schism
Roger II's establishment of the Norman Kingdom of Sicily in 1130 was the foundation for the kingdom's continued development under his successors
Bernard of Clairvaux's influence on Cistercian expansion in 1130 was directly related to the founding of Fountains Abbey in 1134
